title: PHP MySQL Connect
congratsText: You mastered PHP MySQL Connect - Procedural vs PDO!

sections.title: Three Ways to Connect
sections.lesson:
Three Ways to Connect
PHP offers three methods to connect to a MySQL database. Each is supported in modern PHP, and each has a different style.
MySQLi Procedural
  • Function-based style
  • Uses mysqli_connect()
  • MySQL-only
  • Beginner-friendly
PDO
  • Object-oriented style
  • Uses new PDO()
  • Works with 12+ databases
  • Industry standard
There is also MySQLi OOP, which mirrors the procedural approach in object-oriented syntax. This topic focuses on Procedural and PDO — the two most commonly taught approaches.

quiz.question: Which connection method works with databases beyond just MySQL?
options: MySQLi Procedural, because it is the oldest
MySQLi OOP, because objects are more flexible
PDO, because it supports many database drivers
All three methods support multiple databases equally

sections.title: mysqli_connect() Parameters
sections.lesson:
MySQLi Procedural: mysqli_connect()
mysqli_connect() is the core function for the procedural approach. It takes four parameters and returns a connection resource.
$servername = "localhost"; $username = "root"; $password = ""; $dbname = "school_db"; $conn = mysqli_connect($servername, $username, $password, $dbname);
The four parameters (in order):
  • Host — the server address, usually "localhost"
  • Username — your database user, usually "root" locally
  • Password — empty string "" for local development
  • Database name — the specific database to use
Note: The database name is the 4th parameter. A common mistake is mixing up the order.

quiz.question: What is wrong with this mysqli_connect() call?
options: The database name should come before the password
The host and username arguments are swapped
The password must never be an empty string
mysqli_connect() needs five arguments here

sections.title: Checking a MySQLi Connection
sections.lesson:
MySQLi Procedural: Checking the Connection
Always verify the connection before running queries. Use if (!$conn) to detect failure and mysqli_connect_error() to get the error message.
$conn = mysqli_connect("localhost", "root", "", "school_db"); if (!$conn) { die("Connection failed: " . mysqli_connect_error()); } echo "Connected successfully!";
Key functions:
  • if (!$conn) — TRUE if the connection failed
  • mysqli_connect_error() — returns the error as a string
  • die() — outputs the message and halts the script
Think of this as the safety check at the door — if you cannot get in, there is no point continuing.

quiz.question: What does mysqli_connect_error() return when a connection fails?
options: The MySQL error code as an integer
A string describing the connection error
TRUE to confirm the connection has failed
The name of the database that was missing

sections.title: Closing a MySQLi Connection
sections.lesson:
MySQLi Procedural: Closing the Connection
When done with the database, close the connection using mysqli_close(). Pass the connection variable as the argument.
$conn = mysqli_connect("localhost", "root", "", "school_db"); // ... run queries here ... mysqli_close($conn);
Do you have to close it?
  • PHP closes connections automatically at script end
  • Calling mysqli_close() explicitly is best practice
  • Frees the MySQL server connection slot immediately
  • Important for scripts that open many connections
MySQLi procedural uses mysqli_close($conn). PDO uses $conn = null — we will compare these shortly.

quiz.question: What is the correct way to close a MySQLi procedural connection?
options: Set the variable to null: $conn = null
Call mysqli_close() and pass the connection
Call mysqli_disconnect() with the resource
Just let PHP close it; there is no function

sections.title: PDO: new PDO() and the DSN
sections.lesson:
PDO: new PDO() and the DSN
PDO uses a Data Source Name (DSN) — a formatted string naming the driver and database location. You pass it to new PDO() with credentials.
$servername = "localhost"; $username = "root"; $password = ""; $dbname = "school_db"; // DSN format: "driver:host=HOST;dbname=DBNAME" $dsn = "mysql:host=$servername;dbname=$dbname"; $conn = new PDO($dsn, $username, $password);
DSN breakdown:
  • mysql: — the driver prefix (swap to pgsql: for PostgreSQL)
  • host=localhost — the server address
  • dbname=school_db — the target database name
Switching databases only requires changing the DSN prefix — the rest of your PDO code stays the same.

quiz.question: What is the role of the DSN in a PDO connection?
options: It stores the username and password securely
It identifies the driver, host, and database
It sets the character encoding for all queries
It defines the timeout limit for the connection

sections.title: PDO: try/catch Error Handling
sections.lesson:
PDO: try/catch Error Handling
PDO uses exceptions for errors instead of a boolean check. Wrap new PDO() in a try/catch block to handle failures gracefully.
try { $conn = new PDO( "mysql:host=localhost;dbname=school_db", "root", "" ); $conn->setAttribute( PDO::ATTR_ERRMODE, PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION ); echo "Connected successfully!"; } catch (PDOException $e) { echo "Connection failed: " . $e->getMessage(); }
Key parts:
  • try { } — code that might throw an error
  • catch (PDOException $e) — catches any PDO-specific exception
  • $e->getMessage() — retrieves the error description
  • PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION — tells PDO to throw exceptions on errors

quiz.question: What does this catch block print if the database name is wrong?
options: It prints TRUE because an exception was thrown
It prints the PDO error description as a string
It prints nothing; catch blocks suppress output
It prints the line number where the error occurred

sections.title: PDO: Closing the Connection
sections.lesson:
PDO: Closing the Connection
PDO has no close() function. To close it, simply set the variable to null. This destroys the PDO object and releases the connection.
$conn = new PDO( "mysql:host=localhost;dbname=school_db", "root", "" ); // ... run queries here ... // Close PDO connection $conn = null;
Why null and not a function?
  • PDO is an object — PHP destroys it when no references remain
  • Setting $conn = null removes the last reference, triggering cleanup
  • PHP's garbage collector then frees the MySQL connection
Compare: MySQLi uses mysqli_close($conn); PDO uses $conn = null. Same outcome, very different syntax — a key distinction to remember.

quiz.question: What is the correct way to close a PDO connection?
options: Call pdo_close() and pass the connection object
Set the connection variable to null
Call the connection's own close() method
Use unset() with the PDO variable as argument

sections.title: Side-by-Side Comparison
sections.lesson:
Side-by-Side Comparison
Here is the full connect-and-close pattern for both approaches side by side:
// === MySQLi PROCEDURAL === $conn = mysqli_connect("localhost", "root", "", "school_db"); if (!$conn) { die("Failed: " . mysqli_connect_error()); } // ... queries ... mysqli_close($conn);
// === PDO === try { $conn = new PDO( "mysql:host=localhost;dbname=school_db", "root", "" ); $conn->setAttribute(PDO::ATTR_ERRMODE, PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION); // ... queries ... $conn = null; } catch (PDOException $e) { echo "Failed: " . $e->getMessage(); }
Key differences: MySQLi uses functions + if/die; PDO uses objects + try/catch. MySQLi closes with mysqli_close(); PDO closes with $conn = null.

quiz.question: What does this MySQLi code do if the connection fails?
options: It silently retries the connection twice
It prints the error and stops the script
It throws a PDOException to be caught later
It logs the error and continues running

sections.title: Credentials Best Practices
sections.lesson:
Credentials Best Practices
Never hardcode database credentials in your page files. Store them in a separate config file and include it where needed.
// config.php — store OUTSIDE the public web folder <?php define('DB_HOST', 'localhost'); define('DB_USER', 'root'); define('DB_PASS', ''); define('DB_NAME', 'school_db');
// connection.php <?php require_once 'config.php'; $conn = mysqli_connect(DB_HOST, DB_USER, DB_PASS, DB_NAME); if (!$conn) { die("Connection failed: " . mysqli_connect_error()); }
Security rules:
  • Never commit credentials to version control (Git)
  • Place config files outside the public web root
  • Use a limited-permission DB user in production, not root
  • Always use strong passwords on live servers

quiz.question: Why should database credentials be stored in a separate config file?
options: PHP executes faster when credentials are centralized
Changes apply everywhere and leaks are less likely
It prevents MySQL from rejecting plaintext passwords
It is strictly required for PDO connections to work

sections.title: Which Should You Use?
sections.lesson:
Which Should You Use?
Both MySQLi Procedural and PDO are valid. The right choice depends on your project:
Use MySQLi Procedural when...
  • You are only using MySQL
  • Teaching or learning DB fundamentals
  • Maintaining legacy codebases
  • Code simplicity is the priority
Use PDO when...
  • You might switch databases later
  • Building production applications
  • Your team uses OOP patterns
  • Portability matters
Bottom line: For learning and MySQL-only projects, MySQLi Procedural is fine. For scalable applications that may run on different databases, PDO is the better choice.

quiz.question: Which approach is best for apps that may need to switch databases?
options: MySQLi Procedural, because it is database-agnostic
MySQLi OOP, because objects are more portable
PDO, because it supports multiple database drivers
Either one, since PHP handles switching automatically
